reviews: ["GoT.", "How to correlate different classical texts to modern texts and cinema", "I learned a great deal about epics and empires. We learned a lot", "GGE is a fascinating course that covers a wide range of topics related to the governance of the global economy. Throughout the semester, we delved into the politics of trade, investment, monetary policy, immigration, and environmental policies, using various theoretical approaches and examining historical and contemporary evidence and Professor Gaikwad ad the TA's did an absolutely lovely job of ensure that these topics that were not something I had encounter in an academic way yet were manageable and understandable. What I particularly appreciated about this course was the emphasis on policy-relevant case studies. We were able to apply the concepts we learned to real-world situations and evaluate the effectiveness of different economic policies. The course format was lecture-based, but there were also opportunities for discussion and group work, which I found engaging and thought-provoking. Overall, I found The Politics of the Global Economy to be a challenging but rewarding course. The course content was both comprehensive and relevant, and the delivery was engaging and informative. reviews: ["yes", "The principles of econ!!", "I learned a variety of topics in in microeconomics and macroeconomics.", "I learned a decent amount about both micro and macroeconomics. It was nice to also get an understanding of current events through the lens of economics.", "This course allows me to learn economic knowledge both from the microscopic perspective and macroscopic perspective, green-hand friendly and note-taking friendly. Starting with the supply and demand, moving towards the balance sheet approaching the end of this term. I strongly recommend this course for those whether looking to have a taste of economics or planning to major in Economics. 5/5", "Everything about economics. So much information", "I definitely learned about the principles of economics. It was probably my most interesting class and I learned that I actually like Economics and will pursue this field.", "I learned a broad overview of how ecomics works in theory, plus some insight into the actual application of this theory in the real world.", "Can better understand current events in an economic lens, as well as people's general decision making inclinations from an economic perspective.", "In this course, I learned how a market economy functions to determine the relative prices of goods and factors of production, as well as how it allocates resources. I gained a deeper understanding of the conditions under which markets operate efficiently, and why they sometimes experience fluctuations. I also developed insight into the tools and policies that can be used to control these economic fluctuations, which gave me a broader perspective on the dynamics of real-world economies.", "Basic topics in micro and macro, along with how to use these topics to understand events occurring in real life, like learning tariffs in the macro portion of the course while President Trump was putting up tariffs.", "a lot about economics.", "I have learned so much throughout this course. Noor should be teaching with the goal that his students will WANT to spend that 8 hours on economics outside of class. In contrast, I've been in 3000- level classes advertised as the hardest in the curriculum, but the professors were able to convey information in a way that made the work at the very least appropriate to the class. Doesn't allow phones (fair) or computers (unfair), but does allow iPads and other tablets (very unfair). (I'm not saying that having your phone out is OK, but calling people out in a 200+ person lecture hall is cruel.) Noor is entirely unaccommodating: he doesn't take class suggestions, writes in small print on a blackboard, and doesn't record/post any lecture materials. Participation and attendance are mandatory. Yes, even when the class has more than 200 people. Missed class because you were quarantined? Good luck finding lecture notes. During the last lecture, Noor went on a rant about how we're all selfish, and that we should try to minimize our negative impact on the world. Problem sets are riddled with errors and inconsistent in difficulty, but they're always long. The midterm was so long that no one left the room before the time was up. Noor explained that it was \"long and fair\", just like life. reviews: ["I learned more from the TA than I did the professor.", "I learned a lot about microeconomics since I already had a good previous knowledge of macro.", "From the syllabus: Supply and demand; Elasticity; consumer theory; inputs and costs (how firms decide how much to produce in competitive markets); oligopoly; monopoly; market externalities (with special emphasis on climate change and COVID); GDP and CPI; Unemployment and inflation; income and expenditure; Aggregate Demand and Aggregate Supply; Fiscal Policy; Money, banking; Monetary Policy", "basics of economics - micro and macro", "Micro and Macro economics", "Economics graphs and maths", "I learned about micro, macro, policy, and supply and demand.", "I learned the basics of micro and macro economics as well as fundamental formulas and concepts for economics.", "basic principles of micro and macro", "The course is appropriately named as it covers the basic principles of economics. Micro and Macroeconomics are covered in two sections. We also had a particular set of classes on externalities relating to the pandemic and climate change.", "Learned microeconomics and macroeconomics", "Principles of macro and micro economics", "That some professors just don't care at all. That's Prajit Dutta. I did learn a good foundation for macro and micro economics but a lot of that came from just reading the textbook and watching Khan Academy. This class is basically AP Macro and Micro all over again.", "The basic of micro and macro economics.", "I learned the basics of economics and time management with this course!", "The fundamentals of microeconomics and macroeconomics.", "Microeconomics Macroeconomics", "How micro and macro economics work in general terms", "I learned many introductory concepts to understanding the economy, from a macro perspective as well as from the perspective of individuals or firms.", "Learned an overview of micro and macro economics, including ways to graph the supply and demand of a firm/market, government policies that could alter the demand curves of a firm/market, and the factors that can alter the demand and supply curves of a market/firm.", "Quantitive skills.", "Overview of microeconomics and macroeconomics, types of markets/policies/graphs", "It's a principles of economics class so we learned the general introduction to micro and macro as well as some extra content on the externalities of climate change and covid.", "We learned the principles of supply demand, oligopoly, monopoly and other principles related to economics.", "Basic Macroeconomics and Microeconomics", "Basic concepts of micro and macroeconomics, similar to the curriculum of the AP Micro and Macro courses.", "basic economic principles, both micro and macro", "Micro and macro", "In these TA sessions, I was able to further understand principle economic concepts that were taught in class.", "I was able to learn to review and develop the content of the class. 1 Columbia University: Arts & Sciences Fall 2021 Course: ECONUN1105_002_2021_3-PRINCIPLESOFECONOMICS : ECONUN1105_002_2021_3 - PRINCIPLES OF ECONOMICS Instructor: Prajit Dutta", "If you enjoy economics (or just explaining it to other people), this course will make you feel like the smartest person you know. You will suddenly have the confidence to ask absurd questions during class and make redundant statements. If you were on the fence about Econ, it is important to take this course so you know definitively which side of said fence you are on. It will be very clear very soon.", "The class was a good introductory course, I just didn't enjoy my time IN the class.", "Not the class for anyone looking to really pique their interest in the field of economics.", "Take Dutta if he is teaching! Smart man, engaging lectures, lighter workload than Gulati.", "There may be better classes for learning economics as this is a large lecture course and the professor engagement in marginal.", "I liked our textbook, and that's about it. It's how I did 90% of my learning for this class, the other 10% being the problem sets where I would try to apply what I learned from the textbook. The lectures were not very helpful because sometimes we'd get caught up on one question and spend the whole class going over that or the professor would spend a long time over-explaining one concept. I though I was doing okay in the course up until the midterm, which did NOT at all reflect what we had learned up to that point. I studied so hard and thought I understood most if not all of the concepts from the textbook, lectures, and problem sets, only to be met with a midterm full of trick questions. I've since decided to pass/fail the course which is a shame since I find Economics pretty interesting in general and would have liked to feel like all the work I was putting into the class was amounting to something higher than an F on the midterm and probably a C overall in the class. Problem sets weren't too bad but I with there was a little more similarity in how the ps were written and how the midterm was written (ps questions were easy, midterm questions were super hard)", "You have to take it if you want to do anything with Econ in the future so I'd recommend it just on the basis of future studies", "The course material was rather interesting and not difficult, but the midterm was extremely hard to pass.", "This course is amazing, would recomend to anyone interested in economics.", "The course load is fair but the exams and grading is not which can make it really hard for a student to keep focusing in the class.", "Professor Dutta is a fine lecturer. My only problem with this course was the difficulty of the midterm exam (the avg was a 48% and is worth 35% of our grade). Now when I see a meme with the caption \"Basic Economics,\" I forcefully blow air out of my nose because I understand it.", "Basics of micro and macro .", "Professor Gulati is a very involved and passionate professor. The class is very large, but he make a very conscious effort to get to know students, and there are lots of opportunities outside class for professor-student engagement. I think he is a great lecturer and explains concepts in an engaging way. However, I think sometimes his lecture style might not work well for everyone. The lectures are primarily oral with some chalkboard writing, so the information is not really visually organized, which can be challenging. Additionally, any slides used in lecture or recitation are not made available to students which makes sessions stressful. Because we don't have time to copy down notes, students have tried to take pictures, but that is not allowed either. I think there are a lot of great things about this class, and if you do well with the lecture style I would definitely recommend, but I don't feel like students were given all the tools to be successful, which was frustrating. This was especially difficult before exams, there was a review session outside of class time, but if you couldn't make that one session for any reason there were no slides or references available. There are also 6 pop quizzes, with no makeups allowed. It added unnecessary stress to the daily class session, and if you ever have to miss class, it didn't matter the reason you would just get a zero on the quiz. course_title: I learned about the fundamental concepts and principles of macroeconomics and microeconomics. Macroeconomics covers topics such as GDP, inflation, unemployment, fiscal policy, and monetary policy, while microeconomics covers topics such as supply and demand, market equilibrium, consumer behavior, and production theory. You may have also learned how these two fields of study are interconnected and how they impact each other. - The basic principles of economics as covered by other sections - basic macro and micro topics - Basic principles of micro and macroeconomics. - basics of micro and macro - I learned that capitalism is alive and well in the Columbia classroom. - I learned a lot about economics, and how decisions are made using strategic methods. We learned about different economists theories and understanding how the economy works both locally and worldwide. In depth explanations of supply and demand and the causes of their shifts. 1 Columbia University: Arts & Sciences Spring 2023 Course: ECONUN1105_001_2023_1-PRINCIPLESOFECONOMICS : Principles of Economics-Sections 1&2

What I mean by that is the class is not application based, but rather uses applications to solidify the concepts. Whether going over cases and examples of real world applications to our subject in class, or completing problem sets whose questions are based on inspired hypotheticals. This, in turn, allows us to strengthen our reasoning skills and our ability to apply what we learn in class to everyday life.", "How individuals and companies optimize. Learned about supply and demand curve, how individuals and firms make decisions to buy or produce", "What an awful class. Seems to me that Noor has a chip on his shoulder because he knows he's not Gulati. While this should be an opportunity to offer a curriculum that interests students in economics and presents the course material in a fun and engaging way, Noor does the opposite by trying to intimidate his students, telling 1 Columbia University: Arts & Sciences Fall 2021 Course: ECONUN1105_003_2021_3-PRINCIPLESOFECONOMICS : Principles - Fall 2021-ECONUN1105_003_2021_3 - PRINCIPLES OF ECONOMICS Instructor: Waseem Noor them that his class \"won't be an easy A\", and that you'll have to spend 8 hours a week on homework. It's true, you'll have to spend hours of your time wrestling with problem sets that reference things totally unrelated to lecture. Noor should be teaching with the goal that his students will WANT to spend that 8 hours on economics outside of class. This class is not inherently supposed to be difficult (I'm in SEAS and this was a painful technical degree requirement). In contrast, I've been in 3000- level classes advertised as the hardest in the curriculum, but the professors were able to convey information in a way that made the work at the very least appropriate to the class. Doesn't allow phones (fair) or computers (unfair), but does allow iPads and other tablets (very unfair). He has called out several students to the entire lecture hall for taking their phones out during class. (I'm not saying that having your phone out is OK, but calling people out in a 200+ person lecture hall is cruel.) Noor is entirely unaccommodating: he doesn't take class suggestions, writes in small print on a blackboard, and doesn't record/post any lecture materials. Participation and attendance are mandatory. Yes, even when the class has more than 200 people. Missed class because you were quarantined? Good luck finding lecture notes. During the last lecture, Noor went on a rant about how we're all selfish, and that we should try to minimize our negative impact on the world. Problem sets are riddled with errors and inconsistent in difficulty, but they're always long. The midterm was so long that no one left the room before the time was up. Noor explained that it was \"long and fair\", just like life. reviews: ["I learned more from the TA than I did the professor.", "I learned a lot about microeconomics since I already had a good previous knowledge of macro.", "From the syllabus: Supply and demand; Elasticity; consumer theory; inputs and costs (how firms decide how much to produce in competitive markets); oligopoly; monopoly; market externalities (with special emphasis on climate change and COVID); GDP and CPI; Unemployment and inflation; income and expenditure; Aggregate Demand and Aggregate Supply; Fiscal Policy; Money, banking; Monetary Policy", "basics of economics - micro and macro", "Micro and Macro economics", "Economics graphs and maths", "I learned about micro, macro, policy, and supply and demand.", "I learned the basics of micro and macro economics as well as fundamental formulas and concepts for economics.", "basic principles of micro and macro", "The course is appropriately named as it covers the basic principles of economics. The macro half provided a larger scale of supply and demand, by looking at very large firms and institutions, like banks, countries, and governments. Now when I see a meme with the caption \"Basic Economics,\" I forcefully blow air out of my nose because I understand it.", "Basics of micro and macro .", "Professor Gulati is a very involved and passionate professor. The class is very large, but he make a very conscious effort to get to know students, and there are lots of opportunities outside class for professor-student engagement. I think he is a great lecturer and explains concepts in an engaging way. However, I think sometimes his lecture style might not work well for everyone. The lectures are primarily oral with some chalkboard writing, so the information is not really visually organized, which can be challenging. Additionally, any slides used in lecture or recitation are not made available to students which makes sessions stressful. Because we don't have time to copy down notes, students have tried to take pictures, but that is not allowed either. I think there are a lot of great things about this class, and if you do well with the lecture style I would definitely recommend, but I don't feel like students were given all the tools to be successful, which was frustrating. This was especially difficult before exams, there was a review session outside of class time, but if you couldn't make that one session for any reason there were no slides or references available. There are also 6 pop quizzes, with no makeups allowed. It added unnecessary stress to the daily class session, and if you ever have to miss class, it didn't matter the reason you would just get a zero on the quiz. course_title: I learned about the fundamental concepts and principles of macroeconomics and microeconomics. Macroeconomics covers topics such as GDP, inflation, unemployment, fiscal policy, and monetary policy, while microeconomics covers topics such as supply and demand, market equilibrium, consumer behavior, and production theory. You may have also learned how these two fields of study are interconnected and how they impact each other. - The basic principles of economics as covered by other sections - basic macro and micro topics - Basic principles of micro and macroeconomics. - basics of micro and macro - I learned that capitalism is alive and well in the Columbia classroom. - I learned a lot about economics, and how decisions are made using strategic methods. We learned about different economists theories and understanding how the economy works both locally and worldwide. In depth explanations of supply and demand and the causes of their shifts. 1 Columbia University: Arts & Sciences Spring 2023 Course: ECONUN1105_001_2023_1-PRINCIPLESOFECONOMICS : Principles of Economics-Sections 1&2

What I mean by that is the class is not application based, but rather uses applications to solidify the concepts. Whether going over cases and examples of real world applications to our subject in class, or completing problem sets whose questions are based on inspired hypotheticals. This, in turn, allows us to strengthen our reasoning skills and our ability to apply what we learn in class to everyday life.", "How individuals and companies optimize. Learned about supply and demand curve, how individuals and firms make decisions to buy or produce", "What an awful class. Seems to me that Noor has a chip on his shoulder because he knows he's not Gulati. While this should be an opportunity to offer a curriculum that interests students in economics and presents the course material in a fun and engaging way, Noor does the opposite by trying to intimidate his students, telling 1 Columbia University: Arts & Sciences Fall 2021 Course: ECONUN1105_003_2021_3-PRINCIPLESOFECONOMICS : Principles - Fall 2021-ECONUN1105_003_2021_3 - PRINCIPLES OF ECONOMICS Instructor: Waseem Noor them that his class \"won't be an easy A\", and that you'll have to spend 8 hours a week on homework. It's true, you'll have to spend hours of your time wrestling with problem sets that reference things totally unrelated to lecture. Noor should be teaching with the goal that his students will WANT to spend that 8 hours on economics outside of class. This class is not inherently supposed to be difficult (I'm in SEAS and this was a painful technical degree requirement). In contrast, I've been in 3000- level classes advertised as the hardest in the curriculum, but the professors were able to convey information in a way that made the work at the very least appropriate to the class. Doesn't allow phones (fair) or computers (unfair), but does allow iPads and other tablets (very unfair). He has called out several students to the entire lecture hall for taking their phones out during class. (I'm not saying that having your phone out is OK, but calling people out in a 200+ person lecture hall is cruel.) Noor is entirely unaccommodating: he doesn't take class suggestions, writes in small print on a blackboard, and doesn't record/post any lecture materials. Participation and attendance are mandatory. Yes, even when the class has more than 200 people. Missed class because you were quarantined? Good luck finding lecture notes. During the last lecture, Noor went on a rant about how we're all selfish, and that we should try to minimize our negative impact on the world. Problem sets are riddled with errors and inconsistent in difficulty, but they're always long. The midterm was so long that no one left the room before the time was up. Noor explained that it was \"long and fair\", just like life. reviews: ["I learned more from the TA than I did the professor.", "I learned a lot about microeconomics since I already had a good previous knowledge of macro.", "From the syllabus: Supply and demand; Elasticity; consumer theory; inputs and costs (how firms decide how much to produce in competitive markets); oligopoly; monopoly; market externalities (with special emphasis on climate change and COVID); GDP and CPI; Unemployment and inflation; income and expenditure; Aggregate Demand and Aggregate Supply; Fiscal Policy; Money, banking; Monetary Policy", "basics of economics - micro and macro", "Micro and Macro economics", "Economics graphs and maths", "I learned about micro, macro, policy, and supply and demand.", "I learned the basics of micro and macro economics as well as fundamental formulas and concepts for economics.", "basic principles of micro and macro", "The course is appropriately named as it covers the basic principles of economics. The macro half provided a larger scale of supply and demand, by looking at very large firms and institutions, like banks, countries, and governments. Now when I see a meme with the caption \"Basic Economics,\" I forcefully blow air out of my nose because I understand it.", "Basics of micro and macro .", "Professor Gulati is a very involved and passionate professor. The class is very large, but he make a very conscious effort to get to know students, and there are lots of opportunities outside class for professor-student engagement. I think he is a great lecturer and explains concepts in an engaging way. However, I think sometimes his lecture style might not work well for everyone. The lectures are primarily oral with some chalkboard writing, so the information is not really visually organized, which can be challenging. Additionally, any slides used in lecture or recitation are not made available to students which makes sessions stressful. Because we don't have time to copy down notes, students have tried to take pictures, but that is not allowed either. I think there are a lot of great things about this class, and if you do well with the lecture style I would definitely recommend, but I don't feel like students were given all the tools to be successful, which was frustrating. This was especially difficult before exams, there was a review session outside of class time, but if you couldn't make that one session for any reason there were no slides or references available. There are also 6 pop quizzes, with no makeups allowed. It added unnecessary stress to the daily class session, and if you ever have to miss class, it didn't matter the reason you would just get a zero on the quiz. course_title: I learned about the fundamental concepts and principles of macroeconomics and microeconomics. Macroeconomics covers topics such as GDP, inflation, unemployment, fiscal policy, and monetary policy, while microeconomics covers topics such as supply and demand, market equilibrium, consumer behavior, and production theory. You may have also learned how these two fields of study are interconnected and how they impact each other. - The basic principles of economics as covered by other sections - basic macro and micro topics - Basic principles of micro and macroeconomics. - basics of micro and macro - I learned that capitalism is alive and well in the Columbia classroom. - I learned a lot about economics, and how decisions are made using strategic methods. We learned about different economists theories and understanding how the economy works both locally and worldwide. In depth explanations of supply and demand and the causes of their shifts. 1 Columbia University: Arts & Sciences Spring 2023 Course: ECONUN1105_001_2023_1-PRINCIPLESOFECONOMICS : Principles of Economics-Sections 1&2

I know how to calculate probability, determine correlations, perform t-tests and calculate z-scores, two-tailed tests. I understand the different types of decsion-making models (among them probabilistic and non-probabilistic), and how they can be applied to different situations. At the beginning of the course it was not clear to me why I had to suffer the agony of learning the formulae and the material seemed to be too abstract to be relevant to this course of study, As the semester progressed, I came to understand better its use.", "I learned a lot about basic statistical analysis, how to approach analysis, and how to assess whether an analysis was done correctly. reviews: ["Decision Making Theory, How to use R for statical analysis", "In this course I learned about probability, statistics and decision theory. Furthermore, I learned how to implement these methods using R.", "Basic R skills, quantitative/ statistical theories that are used to guide climate decision making and forecasts", "I learned several statistical skills and decision management essentials that I believe will be my biggest take away from the semester", "R-studio", "nothing", "Basic of statistics and then using that knowledge to apply it to climate models and trends in general.", "We learned basic statistics with a lens of climate data analysis. We learned the basics of using R in lab. We learned how to understand probabilities, how to do regression analyses, how to make probabilistic decisions, and how to understand changing probabilities based on forecasting.", "Statistical analysis, R, Bayesian decision theory, probabilistic and non-probabilistic decision theory", "I learned a lot during this class - I had no real background in statistics so this class taught me a lot about applied data analysis, decision modeling, etc. I also did not know how to code prior to this class.", "- Understand basic knowledge in quantitative statistique. Quite useful to decipher and understand scientific articles related to climate change", "I learned a lot about statistics! This is my third year of higher-education statistics but it was the first year I actually learned and grew as a student. I learned about EMV, utility, risk, probabilities, permutations and combinations, climate predictions, data distribution types, etc.... it was a LOT.", "I learned how to perform statistical analysis for climate-related decision-making problems and gained an introduction to RStudio.", "I learned the basics of using R, probabilities, decision rules.", "Stats", "Very practical skills. I learned how to use R studio to play with data.", "Bayes, Insurance, risk, maximin, maximax, minimax, lots and lots of math and coding!!", "I learned statistics with a focus on probability and decision theory. As for skills, my R somewhat improved but not much since it was mostly copy and pasting code.", "I learned how to use R and I learned about probabilistic and non-probabilistic decision models as well as quantitative skills such as running regression, conducting a hypothesis test.", "I learned the basics of statistic as they apply to climate information and decision making under climate uncertainty. This included simple and compound probabilities, hypothesis testing, various distributions and their properties, regression, and probabilistic and non-probabilistic decision rules and analysis. I also gained perspective on error and uncertainty in quantitative analysis, and how to find and avoid misleading statistics using this knowledge.", "How to evaluate, compare, and analyze climatological data using a variety of tools and techniques. We used the program R to do this in lab. In lecture we got a very detailed course on statistics as particularly applicable to climate change data.", "I appreciated the course because it allowed me to broaden my depth and understanding of statistics through a climate science perspective.", "I can recommend to the person who can get the skill of scientific analysis for climate data.", "I did not particularly enjoy this class; however, I do think its important for climate practitioners to at least be exposed to quantitative decision making.", "Some of the topics were explained in too much detail, which was detrimental for some of the class. For example, a lot of detail was given into the derivations of different equations.", "n/a", "I think I retained 10%. Such a disappointment.", "Be ready to work all along the semester, sometimes hardly.", "I'd recommend for someone interested in climate statistics.", "More review time, more TAs", "I only took this class because it was required for the Climate & Society MA. Non C&S students, I do not recommend this course.", "I recommend because of how the professors care about student learning, yet I do think the course could be restructured to help teach the basics of R. Moreover, often Professor Simon would try to cover complex applications of the theories/course concepts he was teaching instead of just the basics which got confusing when I did not understand the general concept."]

reviews: ["American Politics", "In this course, I learned a great deal about the foundations of American democracy and the evolution of federalism within the United States. In addition, I learned key political strategies and tactics that are used and analyzed to champion elections.", "Information about collective action and federalist papers were clearly explained.", "Different proponents of American politics, like voting behavior and the Federalist papers.", "To view politics through a different lens.", "i learned a lot about the history/foundations of American politics as well as American politics today", "Basics of american politics: history, branches of govt, driving factors and why things are the way they are", "I learned about american insitutions and political system.", "History of American Politics, how collective action problems play out in American Politics, the basic forces at work in American Politics", "This class provides a great introduction to the rest of the American political science education at Columbia. The lecture is well structured and the exams test highly relevant knowledge. I deeply appreciate the clarity Professor McClellan brings to the lecture content. The class emphasizes memorization as opposed to critical thinking and argumentation. Discussion sections are interesting but largely irrelevant to the assessments. It is a requirement so you might have to take it, but don't expect depth or anything largely interesting. It paves the path for future seminars and courses. Assessments: 3 non-cummulative exams. Grading: depends on TA.", "I learned much about the American political system.", "I learned why certain institutions are structured in certain ways. I also learned why it is difficult for the three branches to act collectively.", "I learned time management, basic political knowledge, and discussion skills.", "Basic knowledge regarding the structure and mechanisms of American politics. Can be fun and engaging with the right prof, McClellan is stellar. reviews: ["This course helped me develop foundational skills in listening, speaking, reading, and writing Chinese. I learned to comprehend simple questions, short dialogues, and passages, and I became somewhat comfortable with everyday conversations. Writing traditional Chinese characters, understanding stroke order, and constructing basic essays have been particularly rewarding. Additionally, I gained insights into the structure of the Chinese language and cultural nuances.", "I learned beginners Chinese", "I learned so many new grammar structures, basic vocabulary for people/family, food, class, etc. We touched on some cultural/linguistic moments as well. This class is very informative and while you are responsible for keeping up with new vocabulary/structures with each new class, it's been very enjoyable in my opinion. There is a good balance between practicing reading, writing, listening, and speaking in the course.", "This course is ideal for anyone starting to learn Chinese. Chen Wu\u2019s teaching style is exceptionally effective, even for students who might initially struggle. She is dedicated to ensuring that every student builds a solid foundation in the language.", "I think next semester it might be helful to get more homework or optional homework for each day with a few sentences from the powerpoint of that day just to practice. I wish there were more opprtunities to practice wriitng and directly using the sentence structures, so optional practice assignments would be helpful!", "I would absolutely recommend this course to someone who is *interested* in learning Chinese. The structures you'll learn will certainly help you understand certain Chinese words and phrases which come up in the real world/media/etc. I feel more confident with my Chinese now than I did before starting this course. However, I wouldn't take this class for an easy A; that's not what it's here for. I would highly recommend taking Wu Laoshi's course specifically. I sincerely enjoyed my time with Wu Laoshi: she is an incredible teacher who goes above and beyond in many ways to help you learn Chinese effectively and have fun while you're at it. She is very knowledgeable and will very kindly answer any and all questions you have. She always responds to emails quickly which I really appreciated. At the beginning of the year, she made an effort to memorize everyone's names before they entered the room; I don't think I've ever had a teacher before who's done that. Little details like that show that your teacher truly cares about you, made possible by the the very small class size of 10-15 people. I feel that there's a lot of personal attention and care taken to facilitate our learning and help us practice in class. Throughout the semester, Wu Laoshi incorporated details about our lives that we offered voluntarily while practicing using new Chinese phrases and vocabulary, which helped give the class a much more personal and \"human\" feel than just simply reading off vocabulary lists or grammar patterns in a monotone way. She brings humor and good spirits into the classroom, which I feel really sets the tone for the (good) experience you'll have in her class. She even brought in moon cakes to celebrate the Mid-Autumn Festival! Again, the little gestures show how invested she is. She's always happy to meet with students during her office hours and will work with you to alternative office hours via Zoom if you can't make the posted times. TLDR: if you are interested in learning Chinese with a great prof, take this course!"]

reviews: ["so much about work and gender in terms of sociology", "I learned about different theories and studies related to gender segregated work and about gender dynamics in the workplace.", "Learned a lot about gender and work! Little leniency during political tension on campus.", "Psets are decently difficult and definitely should be started early, also go to lecture and watch online lectures as well. The midterm will humble you so bad you will question if you have more than two working brain cells and remove any sense of ego you ever thought you had. The test questions are like the pset questions, if the pset questions took anabolic steroids and synthetic psychoactive substances for three decades. The pset questions are like playing high school soccer, and the midterm is like going solo against the entire FC Barcelona 2009-2015 team with Neymar, Suarez and Messi. Taking the midterm felt like going against the entire US military and only having a purple water balloon to defend yourself. Get ready since this is a major requirement and experience David vs Goliath, but the version where you are David and aren't as lucky.", "As a requirement for Chemistry or related majors, there isn\u2019t much to say, since it\u2019s necessary to graduate. As an elective for other majors and Chemistry grad students, I would only recommend the class if coordination compounds and molecules are directly related to your research interests. Group theory and spectroscopy are taught with a focus more on \u201cwhat you need to know to work in the area\u201d, not so much on the fundamental understanding (tho those two topics are very complex, so it\u2019s understandable its not covered). In the second half of the semester, the course has an extreme focus on electron counting and reactivity (not all that fun or useful if you don\u2019t work with coordination compounds or wacky clusters). The reactivity of coordination compounds is certainly very fun and important to know as a chemist, but dealing with multiple long homeworks with dozens of weird electron counting problems is not exciting at all. Additionally, the course follows the standard chemistry department curve, and will force you to memorize the periodic table for exams (an extremely dubious pedagogical choice, as many others in this course). I recommend looking if your interest would not be better served by courses in the chemistry, physics, or engineering departments before choosing to take this course. Maybe even audit it, as prof. Owen is a really good professor and some interesting knowledge may be gained regardless, as Owen is a really good lecturer and is very open to questions. Talking to him during office hours or asking questions after class leads to some really good and in-depth discussions. He is very knowledgeable and is willing to refer questions to extra resources for a better understanding, even if beyond the scope of the course."]

reviews: ["I learned a lot of history which I was previously unaware of surrounding the complexities of work and gender. I enjoyed learning the first few topics since they felt fresh and interesting. Additionally, the class is heavily reliant on in-class participation and is very discussion based. I wish, though, that the room had been different given the setup making it difficult to see everyone when they were talking. I enjoyed hearing about my peers' perspectives an opinions based on their own work experiences and liked the group work that we did in class. Additionally, it was great that there were a range of perspectives and eras covered within the class (including 40s - now).", "I learned a lot about gender differences and their origins and why we have differences and how they perpetuate.", "learn gender Diversity in the workplace, unfair hiring practice, organisation failures and the division of labour in the household,", "We learned about the gendered division of labor, separate spheres ideology, the glass escalator effect, tokens, emotional labor, \"the second shift, Rosie the Riveter, and the women firefighters movement for respect on the job.", "I learned about the history of the gender dynamics in the workplace and also how these can and do infiltrate domestic life.", "I learned a great deal about the specifies of the problems facing women in the workforce as well as getting a beginner's look into the complexities of how those problems arise and how we can begin to solve them.", "Learning about how work relates to gender.", "I learned the terminology sociologists use in multiple types of gendered labor both in the home and workplace: unpaid reproductive labor, second shift, domestic labor, glass elevator, and occupational gender segregation. Especially the last section with the divison of labor in the home and emotional labor, it was interesting to apply what I've learned to what I envision for my future.", "We learned about gendering in the workplace, tokenism, domestic labor, intersectionality of gender and race, emotional labor.", "I learned a lot because this was my first sociology course. We dove into disparities in income, glass escalator, different fields such as nannies, babysitters, flight attendants, teachers, etc.", "personal reflection in tandem with academic concepts", "I learned about the way that gender influences the home and the labor market.", "We delved into how gender shapes our experiences in the workplace. It was eye-opening to explore how societal norms influence everything from job opportunities to promotion prospects. Learning about the historical context helped me understand why certain inequalities persist today. We didn't just study theory; we also looked at real-world examples and discussed policy implications. Through debates and group projects, I learned to articulate my views and advocate for change. Plus, reflecting on my own experiences and those of others deepened my understanding of the complex intersection of gender and work. Overall, the course empowered me to critically analyze workplace dynamics and work towards creating more inclusive environments.", "A lot of my preconceptions about gender and work were challenged. I learned from historical narratives, mixed media, and (of course) sociological theory to understand how the definition of \"work\" has changed over time, and how gender has been factored into it.", "The course material was good and the assement modes are good, i think just more concrete definitive instructions will be good", "I think there's certainly moments of value in it but I got bored very very quickly.", "Interesting and least stressful class", "The workload is not too much if someone follows syllabus instructions well. If you can read, you will at least get a B+. But this is not what I look for in courses at this school. I want to grow academically and intellectually - this class is not it.", "If you were unfamilar with this topic, I would reccomend. However, if you have an understanding of work and gender, as in household labor, feminist movements in work, etc. I felt it only went so far in advancing my interest and knowledge of this topic, and I expected a little more from it. There were some truly facinating readings and discussions, such as the week on paid domestic labor. but I took issue with how often it felt like white middle class women were centered. There were big efforts for intersectional lenses and critiques, but it always felt a little secondary. It seemed when \"women\" was said, it meant white women, and it only ever included the rest of women if their identities were signified before hand. I think this course made a good effort in an intersectional approach, but it has a bit of a way to go to truly achieve a holistic decentering on white middle class as the basis, as well as going beyond second-wave feminism-esque discussions and readings."]

reviews: ["a lot of things about the agriculture sector which I knew nothing about", "A really, really broad overview of the many components of agriculture: financing, dairy, cocoa, sugar, coffee, supply chains, etc.", "I learned all about sustainable agriculture and what that really means! How ag how changed over the years. The current challenges and opportunities. How ag ag ag commodities impact the market. We had a lot of great speakers come in from various parts of sustainable ag as well.", "The main learnings of the course are from the 4 papers and presentation topics. However, these were randomly assigned, so there was less flexibility in the choice of topic.", "I learned a lot about a small subset of topics within sustainable agriculture. I felt it was easy to understand the topics I was assigned, but because the prof didn't lecture, it was really difficult to learn about the topics my peers presented on.", "We spent a lot of time learning about what \"business-as-usual\" (BAU) looks like for various sectors within agriculture (e.g., dairy, international trade, food services). Less time or emphasis was spent/placed on exploring and learning how to integrate sustainability into agriculture; that time was rather spent with talking about why those solutions aren't scalable and/or profitable. While I appreciated the dose of practicality and realism that Professor Kahn injects, I wish we learned more from him and less from each other. The professor is clearly very knowledgeable about the field, but this was overshadowed by the group papers and presentations every 2 weeks, amassing 5-6 students to quickly scramble and cobble together ~20-30 pages of disjointed paper sections and 20-mins of note-reading presentations...so 1 group gets to know a subject a somewhat well, while the other 7 groups of students not take away much from their peers. I honestly worry some of my classmates (especially those whose first exposure to agriculture was this class) walked away with incorrect information. My main hope with this review is the professor and SUMA program seriously restructure this course because a lot of people are interested in sustainable agriculture/food, and that field isn't one of SUMA's strong suits in terms of course representation. So many people were excited to take one of the only sustainable agriculture classes offered...please offer a class that makes the cost worth it!", "This course covers a wide variety of topics at the intersection of agriculture and sustainability, beginning with the basics of soil science, and an overview of the ag industry (growers, inputs, processors, distributors, wholesale, resale, finance). Prof. Kahn breaks students into groups which present on topics each week, covering such topics as ag finance, ag infrastructure, US Farm Bill, commodities trading, aquaculture, ranching, dairy, GMOs and organics, chemicals and pesticides, global trade, ag tech, and more; these are balanced with presentations from a variety of speakers (mostly from finance side) who are investing in ag in some way. You generally learn the most by working in your groups (there were 4 assignments over the course of the semester), and going in-depth in your topic which you then \"teach\" to the other students in a 15-20 minute presentation. I found that the quality of the student presentations really varied; the short time you have to write the research paper and prepare the presentation means some of the quality suffers, but no doubt students put a lot of hours and effort into this class over the course of 12 weeks.", "It was a class where I deep dived into the topics that I had the papers assigned to. The other topics were more of a general information on the topic. Really appreciated the opportunity to go on field trips and the guest speakers were very interesting. It was also a good opportunity to get a lot of presentation practice in. I also appreciated getting to know different people in the class. I got to develop the skills of using new research tools.", "The course touches on an overview of multiple aspects and contributors to agricultural production of feed, fuel, fiber & food systems. Most of the course is actually taught by the students giving bi-weekly presentations with groups and writing corresponding papers on assigned topics within the sector. Student presentations weren't fact checked, so it's a questionable teaching methodology at best.", "Through this course, I was able to achieve all of the learning objectives outlined in the syllabus. I gained a strong understanding of the key topics, which not only expanded my academic knowledge but also provided perspectives and skills that will be valuable in both my personal and professional life."]

reviews: ["Broad timelines of Chinese, Korean, and Japanese dynastic history, and a long timeline of arts in east asia, including potteries, bronzes, tomb art, painting, funerary clothes, handscrolls, calligraphy, landscape painting, screens, decoration art, and other good material.", "We learned a lot about Chinese, Japanese, and Korean history and the role of art in that history (For example, Japanese influence in Korean art because Japan controlled the Korean peninsula for awhile)", "This was my first art history course ever, so I feel like I learned a lot about interpreting pieces of art and how to appreciate them conceptually.", "I learned about the history and variety of Chinese, Japanese and Korean art. The wars, the shifting politics, the cultural influences and exchanges. The language history, the speicificities of each country as well as the common history and culture. Art historical debates and dicourses. Famous art historians. All the readings would explain the history of the art form, the social historical context that the art was created in, placed the art technique/ school in the history and development of the native and imported art forms. Skills - talking like an art historian. Presenting like an art historian. Visual analysis. We developed an \"art hsitorian tool kit\" throughout the semester - basically eveyr other class we were introduced to a major art historian school of thought, approach to art interpretation. etc. Perspectives - I loved learning about Chinese, Japanese and Korean history because it enabled me to view these countries and their people with a greater reverence to aprpeciate the art they have contributed to the world and the art forms they adopted and made their own. I especially loved learning about Chinese Calligraphy, the reading for that class really explained how the chinese have a different way of preserving their culture and transmitting information and culture from generation to generation. That challenegd my frame of thinking which was very narrowly set on Latin langugaes.", "How to be an art historian, especially in terms of art throughout all ages of China, Japan, and Korea's histories.", "She often dealt with a class that wasn't really willing to do the work she assigned. reviews: ["I gained a strong understanding of botany, plant systematics, and developed strong plant identification skills.", "I learned so much about botany and systematics of plants!", "I have leaned an incredible number of skills in this course. Being observant and mindful of minute but important differences in different plant species, hands on field methods, knowledge in plant systematics, the likes.", "We learned a lot about plants. We learned over 150 plant species and over 50 families. In addition, we talked about morphology, evolution, and phylogenetics of plants.", "an extensive and broad overview of plants. key identification factors of plants, general information, and plant morphology", "I learned many things about the plant kingdom both from a systematics perspective as well as a more \"natural\" fieldwork perspective. You are tasked with deeply learning several plant families, their economic importance, and their identifying features. Students also have to complete an herbarium plant collection, and identify them.", "I learned so much about plant life histories and evolution!", "EVERYTHING about plants. There are two real sections. The morphology and evolution of plants (whole plant family tree) and then there is the field botany portion (200+ species on sight, how to collect plants, how to identify plants)", "Identification of the majority of the plants in our flora, a generic overview of the systematics of plant life, and plant morphology and phylogeny.", "This course covers everything you could possibly want to know about botany. If you want to know more about plants, their evolutionary history, and how to identify them, this class is perfect for you. It also offered me one of the greatest learning environments I've ever had and likely ever will have. Our weekly trips out into the field brought everyone together and we all constantly had a great time learning, so much so we often forgot we weren't just friends out on a hike. There is no other class where you will learn so much while having this much fun.", "I would recommend this class to anyone who is willing to commit to the time commitment of the class.", "This is a good course, but there is so much information. If you fall behind, you may be a goner if you don't catch up quick", "I cannot recommend this class enough. It was a crazy amount of work, but it truly changed the way I look at the world. With such a small class setting, I was really able to get close to my classmates and geek out over plants. Only take this class if you think you would be interested in plants, as you will spend an absurd amount of time with them. reviews: ["Arts in China Japan and Korea", "I learned a broad overview of art history in East Asia, how to discuss and analyze it in conceptual terms and how it fits into a global canon of art.", "Overall history of China, Korea and Japan through the lens of Art.", "To actually believe the warnings of previous reviews for classes. HEED MY WARNING!", "Learned the general history of China, Japan, and Korea from pre-historic times until current day, focussing on art evidently however seeing how both religious and political movements impacted the type and forms of art which were created.", "I learned how to critically analyze how we consume art, and how the way we react to it is informed by our cultural, social, and political context. I can now effectively question the way art is written about from different perspectives and how art is affected by the ways is shown, produced, and consumed. I learned how political events in East Asia and dynamics between the East and West influenced the development of East Asian (and Western) art.", "We learned many different styles of East Asian art, across different cultures and mediums.", "I had wished there had been more about Korean art but I understand it's an academic limitation due to the nature of studying Korean art history. I also wish there had been a discussion about the legacy of Japanese sexual slavery under Japanese colonization and the representations of women's bodies in art from that time.", "If you are interested in art and history, I recommend this class. You will learn about China, Korea and Japan's history through looking at their art.", "The class has so many different assignments and components that you never have any idea what to focus on (as in, three papers, three 20-pg readings for each class, weekly discussion posts, midterm exam, group presentation, final project/paper, final presentation, AND a final exam). Like, I've probably experienced the most difficult and time-consuming classes at Columbia, and this still took the cake for most work. I think what really got to me was how useless a lot of the work was (ex. the group presentation, weekly discussion posts, 'discussion'-based exams, etc.), so that you became so bogged down in the weeds and had absolutely no time to focus on the important components (ex. the final project/paper, readings, etc.).", "As someone who had taken this course as an art humanities requirement, I reccommend it to anyone looking for an alternative to Masterpieces of Western Art as it provides a lens into art outside of the western world.", "This course completely changed the way I understand art, and the dynamics within the global development of art, coming from a background of only studying Western art.", "The assignments were overwhelming in volume; and followed an irregular pattern that made it very difficult to keep track of what was due when. In other classes I have taken, the syllabus laid out a very clear plan for the big assignments of the course and was one or two pages long, but this course's syllabus was extraordinarily long and convoluted causing a lack of clarity in due dates (given the volume of assignments). If there were fewer assignments that followed a more regular pattern, this would make the syllabus much more streamlined and easier to see the layout for the semester. The syllabus itself was so long and dense that only 5 out of 22 students found the two extra credit assignments that were hidden, and that seems to be a very small percentage of students who managed to read the syllabus of a course. There were a number of assignments that could have easily been omitted such as the group project, and a textual analysis assignment that even the professor admitted that we were poorly prepared for as we as a group did not perform very well. The emphasis on textual analysis throughout the semester, not only having a midterm paper based on textual analysis but also the structure of the midterm and final that focused on critiquing/supporting arguments that were unnecessarily dense and hard to parse, felt quite exclusionary to the individual students taking the course who were not majors in an art related field but were taking the course to fulfill their global core requirement and lacked a background in highly technical artistic analysis."]

reviews: ["Quantum mechanics and the basics of chemistry", "Early chem (stoichiometry, etc.), quantum chemistry, and orbitals", "This is less a general chemistry class as it is a quantum mechanics applied to molecular bonding class. Personally, I enjoyed the material and presentation; we don't cover many sections of the textbooks, but the lectures go into three times more detail.", "This half of the general chemistry course was taught at a fairly rigorous level for an introductory course. We planned to learn some applications to this knowledge, like coordination chemistry and basic organic chemistry, but we didn't have the time at the end of the semester.", "i learned all of the concepts i learned in AP Chem in high school and more on a much deeper and complex level. reviews: ["I learned:", "How to identify a broad range of plant species native to the northeast US.", "How to prepare herbarium specimens.", "How to read a cladogram to evaluate the evolutionary relationship between plant families.", "Plant anatomy, and reproduction.", "I learned so much about the plants of the northwest US and how to approach botany from a systematics perspective.", "I learned how to identify plants based on key morphological traits, as well as how to explain plant families' phylogenetic relationships. I also learned the significance of various families (such as Bromeliaceae producing pineapple!) and gained a deeper context of the vegetation in New York City and surrounding areas.", "I learned approximately 150 genus + species of northeast plants, over 50 plant families and their size, the location of each of those 50 plants on a phylogeny and how they relate to each other, at least 40 new botany vocabulary words, how we taxonomically place species, what berries NOT to eat in the woods, and how to sing some summer camp songs.", "I learned fundamental plant biological knowledge, local and regional species identification, and general systematics principles, as well as current plant phylogenies.", "Good class, go into nature, learn plants. It is worth the large amount of work you will have to put in.", "This course was incredible overall. Professor Palmer created an incredibly positive and supportive classroom environment, and I have made close friendships with nearly everyone in the class which is a first for me at Columbia. As an undergraduate student in Urban Studies, it was structurally very challenging for me to succeed in this course as my work is expected to be at the exact same level as the EEEB graduate students in the same class. In all the other graduate level classes I have taken, there were slightly different expectations for undergraduate and graduate students in terms of grading/workload/time commitments. In the end, I had to elect to pass/fail this class because the workload and grading set unreasonable expectations for me to meet. I think this class could benefit greatly from implementing some sort of structural differentiation between grad and undergrad students.", "I would definitely recommend the course, in my own biased plant-loving opinion. However, I would tell them that they NEED to budget their time effectively in this course and not wait until the last few weeks to finish their major assignments.", "if you have the time for it, this class is a real win. Matt is a great professor and it's truly the most fun I have ever had with a class. You will learn so much and have a great time doing it. HOWEVER, when I say it's a time commitment I am not understating it. There are weekly field trips that are usually 9 am to 5 or 6 pm on Fridays, and multiple overnight camping trips. These are so fun and it's a great time, but not a minor commitment. Furthermore, there are a few quite large assignments on top of the trips that you will have to do including a plant collection project and a long research paper, as well as memorize around 150 genus + species and around 50 families of plants. There are two field quizzes on identifying these species and a three-part final exam on ID and systematics. I would say this class is more like a 5, maybe 6 credit class. However, if you have the ability to take it, you will absolutely not regret the time you put towards the class. I am quite sure I will remember this class forever, and hopefully some of the plants too.", "I would be very clear about the workload and time needed, but otherwise would recommend the class."]

reviews: ["R", "How to read csv file in r, again not much.", "Learned the fundamental functions of R", "How to use R to analyze dataset and build model", "In the APAN 5200 course, I gained a comprehensive understanding of applied analytics, covering both foundational concepts and advanced techniques for data modeling and analysis. The course provided a structured framework for approaching data-driven problems, emphasizing the importance of critical thinking in choosing the right methodologies based on the nature of the data and the objectives of the analysis. Through the study of various regression models, machine learning techniques, and ensemble methods, I learned to balance predictive accuracy with interpretability, a crucial skill for real-world applications. One of the key takeaways was the importance of performance optimization in data analytics. I learned how to identify and address computational constraints, such as processing power and memory, which are essential when working with large datasets. This perspective highlighted the need to use efficient methods and technologies to ensure that analytics workflows remain scalable and effective. The course also introduced me to high-performance computing techniques that can be applied to overcome these limitations, which broadened my understanding of the computational challenges in modern data analytics. The course deepened my understanding of various modeling techniques, including linear and logistic regression, decision trees, support vector machines, and ensemble methods like bagging, boosting, and random forests. I learned how each of these models operates, their strengths and weaknesses, and when to apply them based on the context. More importantly, I developed the ability to critically evaluate model performance and understand the trade-offs between model complexity and generalizability. This knowledge is crucial for ensuring that models not only fit the data well but also perform reliably on new, unseen data. A significant aspect of the course was understanding the importance of feature selection and the bias-variance tradeoff. I learned how to reduce model complexity by selecting relevant features, thereby improving model stability and interpretability. The discussions on overfitting and strategies to mitigate it through techniques like cross-validation were particularly insightful. These lessons emphasized the importance of building models that strike a balance between accuracy and robustness, preparing me to tackle real-world challenges where data can be noisy and unpredictable. The course also encouraged a thoughtful approach to analytics, reinforcing the importance of aligning technical solutions with business objectives. I gained a broader perspective on how analytics can drive decision-making and the need to communicate findings clearly and effectively to stakeholders. This emphasis on interpretability and transparency highlighted the ethical considerations of analytics, particularly in areas where models can impact people\u2019s lives, such as finance, healthcare, and policy-making.", "How to use R, how to use different methods to create models in R, advantages/disadvantages of various modeling techniques", "Use R code, the foundation knowledge"]

reviews: ["Basics in chemistry.", "Gen chem but it is more than an introductory level course. Savisky goes a haphazard manner of covering chapters which can be intimidating for people who havent done chemistry in a while.", "I definitely learned a lot of chemistry in this class. The topics were similar to those of AP Chemistry in high school (or any introductory chemistry class), but the course went far more in-depth at a faster pace.", "Chemistry", "I learned alot of gen chem topics like organic chemistry, stoichiometry, bonds, solids, and more. For some odd reason Savizky does organic chemistry first which is definitely unique. I also learned how to approach his very trick exam questions.", "I learned a lot of topics including, nuclear chemistry, intermolecular forces, solids, molecular sturctures, quantum numbers, etc.", "I had a lot of previous chemistry knowledge, but one thing I learned a lot about is the Nuclear Chem.", "I learned chemistry at a basic level for future chemistry learning. I feel like I have a very applicable background in chemistry to be used in a future chemistry class.", "really not much. How to study for the unknown", "atoms and molecules, organic naming, electron configuration, stoichiometry, aqueous solution, nuclear solution, nuclear chem, bonds, molecular structure, intermolecular forces, etc.", "I learned about chemical reactions, how to balance them, atomic theory, nuclear chemistry, and in general a basic foundation on chemistry.", "Review the basics of chemistry and take a more in-depth look at some aspects.", "Deeper understanding of basic chemistry concepts", "I learnt about various chemistry concepts, including molecular structure, intermolecular forces, and solids", "I learned about the importance of Chemistry. Professor Savisky explained everything really well and his Power Point format was wonderful.", "Basic chemistry", "This course solidified my understanding of concepts from high school chemistry and deepened my perspective on what science might look like at a higher level. I also felt like my test-taking skills improved a bit while taking this class.", "Basic chem topics such as nuclear chem, quantum models, stoichiometry, bonding, and more. All of which I had to teach myself.", "We learned about a wide range of the different parts of chemistry.", "I learned how to learn independently. Despite chem being a difficult subject for me to grasp, I used to be excited about learning it and was very interested in becoming a materials science major, however, my experience with this professor has crushed those dreams. In chemistry it seems you learn general rules, and then you learn a few exceptions to those rules. Savizky is obsessed with the exceptions. I understand that the exceptions are just as real and important as anything else, but when I spend so long trying to understand the core concepts of chemistry, applying rules, and finally feel proud of my knowledge, and then get questions wrong on the exam because I did not remember in a certain reaction with two elements from a certain period with a certain charge reacts completely differently to any other general possibility, it's very disheartening and frustrating. It feels like he is trying to \"trick\" us on the exam, and I feel that's not what teachers should strive to do. In addition, he often replies to people's questions in class extremely snarky and narcissistically. It's as if he seeing questions as challenges to his personal knowledge, rather than a student simply trying to understand a difficult topic in chemistry. He clearly has a huge ego, and he is allowed that because he is very knowledgable, but he does not need to deflect that onto his students. I feel uncomfortable raising my hand in class because it almost feels like his responses to others are mocking them. He'll crack a joke every once in a while, and they're not so bad, but other than that the class is not engaging at all. He also has a running joke with mocking physicists, and it was a funny bit at first, but for the frequency at which he says it, it just feels like another attempt to protect his fragile ego. Extremely disappointed that this was my first huge introduction into chemistry. If I did not have such a helpful, encouraging TA, I would not have survived this class.", "Most of the units I actually did learn except VBT and some of the structural things.", "Compared to high school chemistry, this class offered a way more in depth explanation of topics which I think will be really helpful for my success.", "I learned gen chem and some organic chemistry for some reason", "I have learned a lot in this chemistry course. Some of the things that I have learned are about elements, bonds, energy releases, orbitals, hybridization, molecular shapes, spectroscopy, etc. I have learned also how to read questions more carefully and efficiently.", "i learned about gen chem 1 topics", "none", "A lot of stuff that I probably won't use (im majoring in cs in seas) 1 Columbia University: Arts & Sciences Fall 2024 Course: CHEMUN1403_003_2024_3-GENERALCHEMISTRYI-LECTURES : CHEMUN1403_003_2024_3 - GENERAL CHEMISTRY I-LECTURES Instructor: Ruben Savizky", "Learned general chemistry. Topics including organic functional groups, spectroscopy, periodic trends, molecular structure, etc.", "chemistry", "I would only take if necessary.", "The time is definitely hard, so be wary of how you may do with that aspect. Otherwise, I would say if you can't get Roy, Savizky is probably the pick as Parkin doesn't seem to really teach concepts, and Beer is not the best.", "Pick another instructor; any of them will do. Your tests will be the hardest, your grading scale will be the most brutal, and you won't be able to share in the struggles or studies of your other classmates.", "As someone interested in Pre-med, I would only recommend taking this course if Highly interested. It is a huge time commitment, and when I told people I wasn't in SEAS (Aka not required to take the class), they looked at me like I was crazy. I would say take it only if you are very committed or required to take the course. As a student who struggles without direct explanation, I had to access all forms of help (tutoring, office hours, advice from the professor) to hopefully pass.", "Lectures are okay, but whether you go or not won't affect your understanding since he just reads off the slides. I think the aktiv homework is way too easy in comparison to exams though and it doesn't prepare you for the exams at all.", "I would only recommend this class to someone who wants to major or minor in chemistry or a related subject, or for someone who already has a decent amount of prior knowledge about the subject. It helped solidify my personal interest in science, but for someone who isn't all that interested in chemistry it would just be overly stressful. I would not recommend it just to fulfill a science credit.", "I did all the practice exams before exams, went to all my TA office hours and almost all of another TAs office hours (Andre every Friday at 9am)- which he can attest to I would be the only one there every time, booked around 2 tutoring sessions a week with CSA, and studied on my own, and still found it difficult to keep up with content and score well on quizzes and exams. There literally isn't much else for me to do. Even those coming in with years of chem experience were struggling desperately, so how do you think I was surviving???? My GPA is insanely important for medical school, and I hope I get an outcome of this course from the work I put in.", "Idk the curve is pretty good (or so I've heard.) But the lectures are very dull and the o chem being included in the course is not needed. The TAs are pretty nice tho", "I would recommend this course to anyone who likes chemistry, and enjoys working on pretty hard problems that require a lot of thinking at times.", "Savizky, although his exams are very difficult, he is probably the best gen chem I professor that Columbia has to offer.", "none", "Professor Savizky is enthusiastic about his teaching which can make learning enjoyable. However, tests are harsh and the curving scheme induces stress.", "too tough"]

reviews: ["Negotiation and facilitation skills.", "Facilitation is a tricky but super handy skill. During this course, as a newcomer, I have gained valuable insights into my strengths and areas for growth. I have picked up practical skills, like how to encourage everyone to participate while keeping discussions on track. I also learned how to listen better\u2014not just to respond but to really understand what people are saying. The tools and techniques we practiced, like brainstorming methods and ways to handle conflict, feel like things I can actually use in real-life situations. One of the biggest takeaways for me is how much facilitation is about creating a space where people feel comfortable sharing their ideas. It is not just about leading but about helping a group work together and feel heard. I have also started thinking more about my communication style and how it affects the group, which has been eye-opening. In general, this course has given me a solid starting point and more confidence to step into facilitation roles in the future. It has shown me that while it can be challenging, it is also a really rewarding skill to develop.", "I learned a lot about myself and my social interactions.
