course_title: ARTS OF CHINA, JAPAN AND KOREA
workload_distribution: {"Much heavier workload": {"weight": 1, "count": 4, "percent": "28.57%"}, "Heavier workload": {"weight": 2, "count": 4, "percent": "28.57%"}, "Similar workload": {"weight": 3, "count": 5, "percent": "35.71%"}, "Lighter workload": {"weight": 4, "count": 1, "percent": "7.14%"}, "Much lighter workload": {"weight": 5, "count": 0, "percent": "0.00%"}, "No basis for comparison": {"weight": 6, "count": 0, "percent": "0.00%"}}
reviews: ["A history of art transformation, changes, and development in China, Japan, and Korea. Different artworks and art periods of China, Japan, and Korea.", "We learned an overview of art history, critique, philosophy, and sociology in all three countries (Japan, China, and Korea) from pre-ancient times to today. The class moves chronologically, and it mainly lectures based with writing and reading done through courseworks for each class. We gained the knowledge to respond to specific elements of art and accurately describe the meaning behind it. We learned he major artists and pieces of each time period, and what those time periods and artistic movements were across time in East Asia. We looked at the interactions between art and Buddhism (and other religions). And we learned the perspectives of each of the three countries and the way they interacted. Lastly, we focused on the power structures and political shifts that brought about the different art we studied in basically every class.", "We learned, as the course title suggests, about art history in China, Japan, and Korea from prehistoric to contemporary art. Because it's a survey course, we moved through each major movement in East Asian art history and spent an equal amount of time covering them all. Dr. Horisaki-Christens set up her modules in such a way that allowed us to understand the transitions between each time period, how they related to each other geographically, historically, politically, and the significance behind the art/artists/movements that we studied.", "How to analyze and acknowledge East Asian culture in art.", "I really like the idea that all the paper and assignments are assigned to build the final project. Very recommended!", "A great class to fulfill core or art huma requirements. A great course to understand the entire arc of all East Asian art.", "Most people might be intimidated by East Asian art especially if that region is beyond your heritage. You really don't need to know anything about East Asia to enjoy this course. You will, I guarantee, feel more fulfilled, and you will be more well-rounded for it. The material is accessible to anybody, the readings and assignments are reasonable. (As with any course, you get out what you put into it. The workload is just right for a humanities course.) Dr. Horisaki-Christens is a wonderful woman, I can't speak highly enough of her and her pedagogical approaches. She makes you learn without you even realizing you're learning or expanding your mind. The way she teaches is inspiring and disarming and thought-provoking. She allows you to think openly, and she nudges gently in her approach, which has been incredibly effective for me and my classmates to being open to the material and embracing foreign concepts. She also is very timely and gives constructive feedback so that you will improve as a writer and a student. I know this probably sounds hyperbolic, but this course, and learning from Dr. Horisaki-Christens, will make you a better person. It has for me. In learning how to view history and people through the lens of art, I became more connected to it (the history, the people, the art). I have better understanding of art and artists and the artistic impulse. I learned more compassion for periods of history that I thought would never care for. And I now am more cognizant of how I view art - to be more self-aware and more conscientious about filtering my preconceived notions of the world. To ask better questions. In this way, I am more open minded. Compassion is a step toward growth for everybody. I reiterate, this course helped me grow as a person."]
hours_per_week_responses: ["Too many", "Weekly reading + discussion post, 2 papers, 2 at-home midterm and final (3 written questions), 1 group presentation (10 mins), 1 final project.", "5-8", "We met twice a week for 1 hour and 15 minutes each lecture. I spent about 7-8 hours every week for the readings and short weekly discussion assignments (but I'm admittedly a very slow reader). There were three trips to the MET, MoMA and other exhibits/galleries, which I did on my own time (I brought family and friends along, so those were fun to do). There are three writing assignments and a final project. The writing assignments are initial inquiries into the final project, so they're all related, and the timing of the due dates helps you start thinking in advance for the final project. Having these assignments also allows you to receive constructive feedback about your final project.", "I think the expectations surrounding the weekly workload-how annotated weekly readings needed to be, that we should be mostly skimming- could have been communicated more clearly at the beginning of class. I also think the amount of work that is assigned in going through the modules, in addition to course readings within the modules, is too much and makes it hard to focus on anything in particular. 2"]

Over the last few decades, social scientists increasingly have investigated how people's relationships with others affect their health, wealth and popularity, among other things. With the growth of the Internet and on-line communities and social media sites, there is more (and more varied) social network data than ever. In this course, we will learn how to think about, analyze and visually display social network data. reviews: ["I honestly didn't learn that much, except for how to memorize a couple basic rules and chemistry facts that would be important for the exams.", "I didn't really learn that much in this course after stoichiometry.", "Learned all basic chemistry topics to a good degree.", "I learned the basics of chemistry including elementary quantum mechanics, organic nomenclature, lewis structures, stoichiometry, molecular structure, and periodic trends.", "Atomic structure, basic nomenclatures, electron configurations and periodic trends, bonding and molecular structure and intermolecular forces", "-stoichiometry, some organic chem, MO, electron configuration, etc", "I guess everything about chemistry? It was interesting to finally do Einstein's theory as he is famously known for e=mc^2 and learn about how different scientists solve problems relating to atoms and elements on Periodic Table. Building blocks: atomic theory, bonding theory, molecular orbital theory, electronic energy levels, ionization energy, electronegativity, and electron affinity, how to read the Periodic Table. Basics of Organic Chemistry: simple and common functional groups, IUPAC naming. Periodic trends and bond characteristics e.g. diamagnetism, d-block element reactivity. Stoichiometry, acid-base reactions, redox (oxidation-reduction) reactions, computation of molar masses. Lewis Diagrams, Resonance in Lewis Structures, VSEPR theory and its limitation, molecular geometry and accurate prediction of said geometry. Basics of Solids, methods of spectroscopy, how to read or analyze spectrometry. Aqueous solutions, ionic equations, net ionic equations. Limiting reactants. reviews: ["I am leaving this class with an excellent foundational knowledge of a wide variety of Post-Freud theory, having had nearly none when I entered it. Adele Tutter, her guest lecturers, and the other students all contributed to making this class a challenging learning environment that also felt like a safe place to share ideas. I looked forward to this class every week.", "I learned so much in this class. It's an unbelievably broad field and any course at this level could just scratch the surface of the different schools of psychoanalytic thought but Professor Tutter (and all of the guest lecturers she brought in, who were all phenomenal) did a fantastic job of giving us frameworks through which to view and compare various theorists. This course did a great job of going in depth on some major thinkers and movements in the field without sacrificing breadth.", "This course surveyed the main strains of thought in psychoanalytic theory after Freud. We read texts from Bion, Laplanche, Winnicott, and others. The course focused both on theoretical and clinical application, and it was quite interesting to see how the two intersected.", "Post-Freudian psychoanalysis. The two major figures that were not covered were Lacan and Carl Jung, and the rationale for this decision was that these figures are often taught in other classes. The focus of this course was to highlight other important figures in psychoanalysis who do not get as much attention, such as Melanie Klein, Loewald, Winnicott, Kohut, Laplanche, and Bion, to name a few. I agree with this decision, but others may not.", "A (rather) comprehensive overview of psychoanalytic theories post Freud. Major developments and theories in object-relations, ego, self-psychology, relational, etc. An introductory course to sets of psychoanalytic ideas that are vast and deep. Prior knowledge to Freud could be helpful", "This course was an excellent overview in many important Post-Freudian theory, from Anna Freud, Melanie Klein, Winnicott, Bion, Fanon, and feminism and psychoanalysis, I learned a great deal at a high level about all of the ways that theorists took Freud beyond Freud and the ways in which those theorists built on, critiqued, and struggled with one another towards types of analysis that might be more historically specific for subjects and patients that Freud may not have been able to theorize or understand--psychotics, children, groups, the colonized and racialized. It helped me identify the thinkers I wish to pursue much more deeply and set me up for success in charting my own educational journey with the rare opportunity to experience a survey course in a seminar style.", "I would recommend this course to anyone wanting to learn about the history of psychoanalysis and contemporary frontiers in the field. You can go into the class with little to no background knowledge and you'll understand what's going on, and any prior knowledge (not just from psychology or psychoanalysis) will be very stimulating to guiding your own learning as well as the class discussions.", "I can't recommend this class enough for anyone interested in psychoanalysis. However, I would not recommend taking this course without taking Professor Tutter's fall semester Freud course first. You're not going to be able to really read the texts unless you have a strong understanding of Freud going into the semester.", "Psychoanalysis theory is rarely taught on its own in academia this is an amazing opportunity to learn from the experts on the topics."]

reviews: ["Social networks", "This class is a broad discussion on how social networks are studied statistically within social science, both in-person like the relationships between people in a class or a law firm as well as digital networks like social media. In its broadest sense, the methods here can be used to study any sort of well-defined interconnected system--even the relationship between links on the internet can be studied in this manner. reviews: ["I learned a wealth of knowledge during this course. Not only did I learn invaluable skills to improve my facilitation skills but I also learned crucial skills for improving my personal skills. I have struggled with mental health most my life and being assertive and setting boundaries has always been a bit difficulty for me. The \"assertive skills\" covered within this course really resonated with me and I look forward to using them to strength my abilities to set boundaries and assert myself.", "This course delivered new insights and applications for my own self-reflection and understanding of others. It was a fun class and a good way to wrap up a month- long intensive.", "I learned interpersonal skills, theories, reflective practices such as active listening and negotiation techniques, received feedback, engaged in self-reflection, and developed mediation skills through lectures, group exercises, videos, and journaling.", "Taking the Interpersonal Dynamics course with Dr. Anderson and Faith this summer was an incredibly impactful experience for me. It really pushed me to deepen my self-awareness and sharpen my skills as a practitioner of collaborative conflict resolution. The face theory and FIRO concepts were also really eye-opening in terms of understanding interpersonal dynamics. I gained insight into my own needs for inclusion, control and openness, and how those drive my behavior. More importantly, I learned to recognize those needs in others and adapt my approach accordingly. In terms of specific skills, the course was incredibly hands-on. The video negotiation project was nerve-wracking but so valuable. Recording myself in a role-play, getting feedback from my peers, and watching the video - that whole process revealed so much about my strengths and areas for improvement. I was able to see my active listening, empathy and assertion skills in action and identify concrete things to work on. The mediation and facilitation practice sessions were also highlights for me. I got to experiment with reframing, redirecting emotional reactions, bridging differences, and keeping discussions on track. Dr. Anderson's debriefs and the readings, like the ones on emotions in conflict, really tied it all together. I came away feeling much more equipped to manage the complexities of multiparty conflicts. Overall, this course was transformative both personally and professionally. It gave me a deeper understanding of myself and others, a toolkit of practical skills, and a roadmap for continued development. I find myself approaching conflicts with a new level of self-awareness, intentionality and confidence. And I know I'm just scratching the surface - reflective practice is a lifelong journey. I'm immensely grateful to Dr. Anderson, Faith, and my classmates for co-creating such a powerful learning environment. The vulnerability, support and challenge in our group discussions took the experience to a whole other level."]

reviews: ["I learned how to do some basic coding in R, which is not a skill that I hoped to get out of this program, but still kind of cool nonetheless.", "I learned a lot about how statistics can be misleading & how to perform crop output functions that take into account internal variability. These skills can be used for climate models!", "I learned statistics and decision modeling in regards to climate decisions", "quantitive climate data analysis and applications", "Decision theory and decision making under uncertainty (and at times certainty). This course gave us a strong and critical lens to analyze data/statistics through.", "I greatly improved in my skills in R as well as decision theory!", "This course taught me coding and problem solving skills. I learned the conceptual knowledge needed to apply to real world problems.", "I learned a lot of new material and I think that for those who do not have a background in statistics, the professors do a great job in providing examples that you can understand.", "quantitative models of climate change, using statistical models and techniques to interpret data", "I became familiar with R and applying statistical concepts to climate change issues like ENSO events and farming decisions", "Basic statistics from correlations, probability, odds, to decision making.", "Took a deep dive into statistics as they relate to climate information. Also learned about different decision analysis methods.", "I learned so much from this course regarding decision theory. My group work in this course was also extremely beneficial.", "I learned that data, and climate data is complex. I learned that there is whole lot of data and a lot of room for interpretation in statistics and that any statistic should be carefully scrutinized. The instructors worked hard to convey real life problems in terms of abstract models and formula. I learned this to a very limited degree. I know how to calculate probability, determine correlations, perform t-tests and calculate z-scores, two-tailed tests. I understand the different types of decsion-making models (among them probabilistic and non-probabilistic), and how they can be applied to different situations. At the beginning of the course it was not clear to me why I had to suffer the agony of learning the formulae and the material seemed to be too abstract to be relevant to this course of study, As the semester progressed, I came to understand better its use.", "I learned a lot about basic statistical analysis, how to approach analysis, and how to assess whether an analysis was done correctly. I also learned about how to quantify climate-related decisions that people must make in the real world. I feel like I'm better able to understand what scientific reports mean\u2013including the IPCC/AR6 assessments\u2013and I have more confidence in my own analytical skills.", "We learned a lot about probabilities, hypothesis testing, forecasting, and R programming. We also learned quite a lot about how to evaluate statistics and interpret our results in ways that can be communicated clearly to the public.", "Statistics applied to climate change, programming in R, basic decision theory", "Having never taken a statistics/R programming course prior, I was introduced to a lot of new concepts. reviews: ["I came into the class knowing quite a bit about this subject from my undergrad, but I learnt a lot from this class in terms of the practical applications and computer side of the class. The prof took advantage of the wide range of knowledge that kids in the class had and this was particularly useful.", "The course was broken down into two \"halves\" - the first covering the philosophy of AI in terms of the philosophy of intelligence, thinking, acting, and related concepts, and the second covering the ethics of AI use in the world today. I had some familiarity with AI coming in but - holy cow! - I learned so much more in this class.", "I got to learn so much about artificial intelligence and where are we as society in technological terms. I got use to reading the classics or philosophers from the enlightenment.", "I gained a significant amount of knowledge regarding the history and technicalities of artificial intelligence (both GOFAI and Neural Networks). I also gained deeper insight into the contemporary ethical issues of AI, such as the regulation of autonomous vehicles and weapons, and algorithmic fairness.", "Philosophic concpets around artificial intelligence technology, I now have a better unerstanding of neural networks, I have a better graps of the ethics of artificial intelligence etc.", "I learned, in a quite exhaustive way, the history and current landscape of AI development. I had much more informed conversation when encountering experts in the field, so this class definitely shaped the way I thought about this topic. Interesting related philosophical questions are explored, though perhaps not to their fullest extent (I think generally that a lot of potentially futurist speculation was kept to a minimum, even though one can imagine such scenarios providing for rich material for philosophy).", "If you want to know about AI, take this course. reviews: ["This course helped me develop foundational skills in listening, speaking, reading, and writing Chinese. Writing traditional Chinese characters, understanding stroke order, and constructing basic essays have been particularly rewarding. Additionally, I gained insights into the structure of the Chinese language and cultural nuances.", "I learned beginners Chinese", "I learned so many new grammar structures, basic vocabulary for people/family, food, class, etc. We touched on some cultural/linguistic moments as well. This class is very informative and while you are responsible for keeping up with new vocabulary/structures with each new class, it's been very enjoyable in my opinion. There is a good balance between practicing reading, writing, listening, and speaking in the course.", "This course is ideal for anyone starting to learn Chinese. Chen Wu\u2019s teaching style is exceptionally effective, even for students who might initially struggle. She is dedicated to ensuring that every student builds a solid foundation in the language.", "I think next semester it might be helful to get more homework or optional homework for each day with a few sentences from the powerpoint of that day just to practice. I wish there were more opprtunities to practice wriitng and directly using the sentence structures, so optional practice assignments would be helpful!", "I would absolutely recommend this course to someone who is *interested* in learning Chinese. The structures you'll learn will certainly help you understand certain Chinese words and phrases which come up in the real world/media/etc. I feel more confident with my Chinese now than I did before starting this course. However, I wouldn't take this class for an easy A; that's not what it's here for. I would highly recommend taking Wu Laoshi's course specifically. I sincerely enjoyed my time with Wu Laoshi: she is an incredible teacher who goes above and beyond in many ways to help you learn Chinese effectively and have fun while you're at it. She is very knowledgeable and will very kindly answer any and all questions you have. She always responds to emails quickly which I really appreciated. At the beginning of the year, she made an effort to memorize everyone's names before they entered the room; I don't think I've ever had a teacher before who's done that. Little details like that show that your teacher truly cares about you, made possible by the the very small class size of 10-15 people. I feel that there's a lot of personal attention and care taken to facilitate our learning and help us practice in class. Throughout the semester, Wu Laoshi incorporated details about our lives that we offered voluntarily while practicing using new Chinese phrases and vocabulary, which helped give the class a much more personal and \"human\" feel than just simply reading off vocabulary lists or grammar patterns in a monotone way. She brings humor and good spirits into the classroom, which I feel really sets the tone for the (good) experience you'll have in her class. She even brought in moon cakes to celebrate the Mid-Autumn Festival! Again, the little gestures show how invested she is. She's always happy to meet with students during her office hours and will work with you to alternative office hours via Zoom if you can't make the posted times. TLDR: if you are interested in learning Chinese with a great prof, take this course!"]

If you take this class you will be heard, worked with, your contributions appreciated, and importantly, you will walk away at the end of the semester blown away by how much you learned, not just about distant histories of a 'mystical Sufism' but about how pertinent this history is to understanding the modern world and the many ways Islam shapes the world today. reviews: ["I learned a lot in this course, expanding my knowledge on Islam generally, and on South Asian history (on which I had had very limited knowledge). I was also exposed to some history of colonialism in South Asia, and post-colonial studies and theoretical concepts, which I had been largely ignorant of before taking this class. Learning about the history of India-Pakistan partition, I was also able to begin to gain an appreciation for what increasingly motivates people in the Muslim world to adopt ideologies which the West pejoratively denotes as 'Islamism'. I understand now that broad denouncements of 'Salafism' are often over-simplifications (at least to some degree within the Pakistani context); many individuals, even those who express militant leanings, also participate in Sufi practices (shrine veneration, dhikr, etc.), and may hold theological views which orthodox Salafists would repudiate.", "This course has provided me with valuable knowledge, skills, and perspectives that have broadened my understanding of Islam and its mystical tradition. Through the study of primary and secondary sources, I developed critical thinking skills and learned how to interpret and evaluate historical sources. Moreover, I gained a deeper understanding of Sufi attitudes toward the body, lineage, power, and religious authority, and gained a broader perspective on the ongoing debates over the place of Sufism within the wider Islamic tradition. Finally, I gained an appreciation for the continued importance of Sufism in the modern world and its relevance to contemporary social, cultural, and political issues. Overall, this course has been a valuable and enriching experience that has deepened my understanding of Islam and its mystical tradition.", "This course not only taught me about main tenets of Sufism, but it also taught me how it has been shaped in South Asia. It gave me a refined understanding of mysticism, love of God, and some of the sad realities of how it has been transformed by hate and greed of humans. The course taught me to reflect on my actions and most importantly introspect.", "I would recommend this course to someone interested in religious studies and/or sociology and postcolonial theory.", "Its a wonderful course which will not only improve your understanding of South Asia but will allow you to read interesting text on music, religion, politics, and culture of South Asia. 1 Columbia University: Arts & Sciences Spring 2023 Course: RELIGU4326_001_2023_1-SUFISMINSOUTHASIA : RELIGU4326_001_2023_1 - SUFISM IN SOUTH ASIA Instructor: Quinn Clark"]
hours_per_week_responses: ["5-7", "5-6 hours weekly", "12 hours per week 2"]

reviews: ["so much about work and gender in terms of sociology", "I learned about different theories and studies related to gender segregated work and about gender dynamics in the workplace.", "Learned a lot about gender and work! From DOL in same-sex households to racial inequality in the workforce, to gender inequality, to trans workers... lots of important topics and it was presented really well", "so much about women's AND mens perspectives/ experiences in work based on their gender, sex, race, class, etc.", "I learned about gender inequalities in the workplace and in contemporary culture.", "I learned a lot about gender discrepancies in different fields. I also learned concepts that I didn't realize existed, but once I realized it it made so much sense.", "In my sociology of work and gender class, I learned about the complexities of the relationship between gender and work in society. One of the key issues we discussed was discrimination, which can manifest in many different ways, such as unequal pay and limited access to leadership positions. Additionally, we talked about the challenges of balancing work and family responsibilities, which can disproportionately affect individuals of certain genders. However, I also learned about the evolution of policies and social norms that aim to address these challenges and promote more equitable opportunities for all individuals in the workforce. Overall, the class provided me with a better understanding of the ways in which gender and work intersect and the ongoing efforts to create more inclusive and diverse workplaces.", "I learned about gender discrimination and inequality in a variety of career fields. This class would be especially interesting for female identifying individuals who have had experiences of prejudice and discrimination in the workplace. We discussed policies in the military, spoke about reproductive and emotional labor, and work in the household.", "I learned so much in this course. reviews: ["A ton", "a lot of inorganic chemistry", "I learned about transition metal chemistry (electron counting, reactivity), how to construct molecular orbital diagrams, how to understand sigma and pi effects, among other things.", "The molecular pictures, spectroscopy and reaction of organic complexes", "MO theory and point groups", "Super useful if going into further chemistry studies or research. Little leniency during political tension on campus.", "Psets are decently difficult and definitely should be started early, also go to lecture and watch online lectures as well. The midterm will humble you so bad you will question if you have more than two working brain cells and remove any sense of ego you ever thought you had. The test questions are like the pset questions, if the pset questions took anabolic steroids and synthetic psychoactive substances for three decades. The pset questions are like playing high school soccer, and the midterm is like going solo against the entire FC Barcelona 2009-2015 team with Neymar, Suarez and Messi. Taking the midterm felt like going against the entire US military and only having a purple water balloon to defend yourself. Get ready since this is a major requirement and experience David vs Goliath, but the version where you are David and aren't as lucky.", "As a requirement for Chemistry or related majors, there isn\u2019t much to say, since it\u2019s necessary to graduate. As an elective for other majors and Chemistry grad students, I would only recommend the class if coordination compounds and molecules are directly related to your research interests. Group theory and spectroscopy are taught with a focus more on \u201cwhat you need to know to work in the area\u201d, not so much on the fundamental understanding (tho those two topics are very complex, so it\u2019s understandable its not covered). In the second half of the semester, the course has an extreme focus on electron counting and reactivity (not all that fun or useful if you don\u2019t work with coordination compounds or wacky clusters). The reactivity of coordination compounds is certainly very fun and important to know as a chemist, but dealing with multiple long homeworks with dozens of weird electron counting problems is not exciting at all. Additionally, the course follows the standard chemistry department curve, and will force you to memorize the periodic table for exams (an extremely dubious pedagogical choice, as many others in this course). I recommend looking if your interest would not be better served by courses in the chemistry, physics, or engineering departments before choosing to take this course. Maybe even audit it, as prof. Owen is a really good professor and some interesting knowledge may be gained regardless, as Owen is a really good lecturer and is very open to questions. reviews: ["This course provided me with a wonderful and in-depth survey of English prose literature written between the years 1600 and 1660, while also providing me with a wealth of historical context, which greatly enriched my understanding of the literary works I studied in this course. For me, one of the most significant skills I was able to improve by taking this course was my proficiency in reading, deciphering, and writing about literature which may be written in an antiquated style (17th-century, Early Modern English), possibly with strange and variable spellings/verbiage. After completing this course, I feel much more confident about my ability to do this, especially after starting this course with essentially no experience reading 17th-century literature other than Shakespeare and other fairly mainstream required readings for other classes.", "I'd recommend this class in general because it showed me a whole body of works and authors I was unfamiliar with, and Dr. Stewart was a hugely knowledgeable instructor. However, the nature of these 17th century texts is to be dense and sort of unpleasant to read, which is why I picked \"probably\" and not \"definitely\" recommend.", "I think students from a variety of disciplines would get something out of it--whether they are studying English, Literature, Linguistics, History, Religion, or Philosophy, there is something relevant here. I learned so much. I highly recommend the course. 1 Columbia University: Arts & Sciences Spring 2024 Course: ENGLGU4263_001_2024_1-ENGLISHLITERATURE1600-1660 : ENGLGU4263_001_2024_1 - ENGLISH LITERATURE 1600-1660 Instructor: Alan Stewart"]
hours_per_week_responses: ["5-10", "4 hours", "I probably devoted roughly 5-6 hours to this course every week, give or take. When working on papers and conducting research and extra reading, it was probably slightly more. 2"]

reviews: ["In class, we read a variety of works from colonialist and post-colonialist India (authored by Rabindranath Tagore, Raja Rao, Gandhi, Ambedkar, Mahasweta Devi, Bapsi Sidhwa, Amitav Ghosh, Arundhati Roy, and more). We discussed issues of caste, gender, Westernization, oppression, trauma, and memory throughout these works. I learned about a variety of literary and political perspectives in 20th century and 21st century India through these works.", "About the field of South Asian Writing in English, about South Asian history, culture, and social dynamics", "Reading and interpreting literature from and after the Indian independence movement. Persistent problems and conflicts in Indian societies.", "We read and analyzed a broad range of novels and short stories by mostly South Asian authors. Many were originally written in English, and some were in translation. We covered topics such as caste, partition, the diaspora, and class."]

reviews: ["I learned a lot of history which I was previously unaware of surrounding the complexities of work and gender. I enjoyed learning the first few topics since they felt fresh and interesting. Additionally, the class is heavily reliant on in-class participation and is very discussion based. I wish, though, that the room had been different given the setup making it difficult to see everyone when they were talking. I enjoyed hearing about my peers' perspectives an opinions based on their own work experiences and liked the group work that we did in class. Additionally, it was great that there were a range of perspectives and eras covered within the class (including 40s - now).", "I learned a lot about gender differences and their origins and why we have differences and how they perpetuate.", "learn gender Diversity in the workplace, unfair hiring practice, organisation failures and the division of labour in the household,", "We learned about the gendered division of labor, separate spheres ideology, the glass escalator effect, tokens, emotional labor, \"the second shift, Rosie the Riveter, and the women firefighters movement for respect on the job.", "I learned about the history of the gender dynamics in the workplace and also how these can and do infiltrate domestic life.", "I learned a great deal about the specifies of the problems facing women in the workforce as well as getting a beginner's look into the complexities of how those problems arise and how we can begin to solve them.", "Learning about how work relates to gender.", "I learned the terminology sociologists use in multiple types of gendered labor both in the home and workplace: unpaid reproductive labor, second shift, domestic labor, glass elevator, and occupational gender segregation. Especially the last section with the divison of labor in the home and emotional labor, it was interesting to apply what I've learned to what I envision for my future.", "We learned about gendering in the workplace, tokenism, domestic labor, intersectionality of gender and race, emotional labor.", "I learned a lot because this was my first sociology course. We dove into disparities in income, glass escalator, different fields such as nannies, babysitters, flight attendants, teachers, etc.", "personal reflection in tandem with academic concepts", "I learned about the way that gender influences the home and the labor market.", "We delved into how gender shapes our experiences in the workplace. It was eye-opening to explore how societal norms influence everything from job opportunities to promotion prospects. Learning about the historical context helped me understand why certain inequalities persist today. We didn't just study theory; we also looked at real-world examples and discussed policy implications. Through debates and group projects, I learned to articulate my views and advocate for change. Plus, reflecting on my own experiences and those of others deepened my understanding of the complex intersection of gender and work. Overall, the course empowered me to critically analyze workplace dynamics and work towards creating more inclusive environments.", "A lot of my preconceptions about gender and work were challenged. I learned from historical narratives, mixed media, and (of course) sociological theory to understand how the definition of \"work\" has changed over time, and how gender has been factored into it.", "The course material was good and the assement modes are good, i think just more concrete definitive instructions will be good", "I think there's certainly moments of value in it but I got bored very very quickly.", "Interesting and least stressful class", "The workload is not too much if someone follows syllabus instructions well. If you can read, you will at least get a B+. But this is not what I look for in courses at this school. I want to grow academically and intellectually - this class is not it.", "If you were unfamilar with this topic, I would reccomend. However, if you have an understanding of work and gender, as in household labor, feminist movements in work, etc. I felt it only went so far in advancing my interest and knowledge of this topic, and I expected a little more from it. There were some truly facinating readings and discussions, such as the week on paid domestic labor. but I took issue with how often it felt like white middle class women were centered. There were big efforts for intersectional lenses and critiques, but it always felt a little secondary. It seemed when \"women\" was said, it meant white women, and it only ever included the rest of women if their identities were signified before hand. I think this course made a good effort in an intersectional approach, but it has a bit of a way to go to truly achieve a holistic decentering on white middle class as the basis, as well as going beyond second-wave feminism-esque discussions and readings."]

reviews: ["Throughout the semester, the course was organized so students can learn a step-by-step Qualitative research methods with assignments along the way. the assignments are creative and useful.", "I learned how to work better in groups and understand different aspects of qualitative analysis.", "The course outlined that I would develop a general understanding of qualitative research, its methods, and their application in clinical and community settings. While I did gain a basic grasp of these concepts, the depth of understanding remained quite limited. We were also told we would operationalize concrete qualitative methods through our group research project; however, this aspect felt rushed and disorganized, with key guidelines provided only late in the semester. Although we were expected to practice crafting semi-structured, in-depth interview protocols and conduct mock interviews in class, opportunities for feedback were minimal. On a positive note, we did complete the promised exercise of coding a publicly available transcript and learned how to derive categories and themes from the coding process. However, we did not gain the expected evaluative skills for using qualitative methods to assess the impact of narrative medicine practice. As a result, I\u2019m still unclear about how qualitative research methods intersect with the principles and practices of narrative medicine."]

reviews: ["a lot of things about the agriculture sector which I knew nothing about", "A really, really broad overview of the many components of agriculture: financing, dairy, cocoa, sugar, coffee, supply chains, etc.", "I learned all about sustainable agriculture and what that really means! We had a lot of great speakers come in from various parts of sustainable ag as well.", "The main learnings of the course are from the 4 papers and presentation topics. However, these were randomly assigned, so there was less flexibility in the choice of topic.", "I learned a lot about a small subset of topics within sustainable agriculture. I felt it was easy to understand the topics I was assigned, but because the prof didn't lecture, it was really difficult to learn about the topics my peers presented on.", "We spent a lot of time learning about what \"business-as-usual\" (BAU) looks like for various sectors within agriculture (e.g., dairy, international trade, food services). Less time or emphasis was spent/placed on exploring and learning how to integrate sustainability into agriculture; that time was rather spent with talking about why those solutions aren't scalable and/or profitable. While I appreciated the dose of practicality and realism that Professor Kahn injects, I wish we learned more from him and less from each other. The professor is clearly very knowledgeable about the field, but this was overshadowed by the group papers and presentations every 2 weeks, amassing 5-6 students to quickly scramble and cobble together ~20-30 pages of disjointed paper sections and 20-mins of note-reading presentations...so 1 group gets to know a subject a somewhat well, while the other 7 groups of students not take away much from their peers. I honestly worry some of my classmates (especially those whose first exposure to agriculture was this class) walked away with incorrect information. My main hope with this review is the professor and SUMA program seriously restructure this course because a lot of people are interested in sustainable agriculture/food, and that field isn't one of SUMA's strong suits in terms of course representation. So many people were excited to take one of the only sustainable agriculture classes offered...please offer a class that makes the cost worth it!", "This course covers a wide variety of topics at the intersection of agriculture and sustainability, beginning with the basics of soil science, and an overview of the ag industry (growers, inputs, processors, distributors, wholesale, resale, finance). You generally learn the most by working in your groups (there were 4 assignments over the course of the semester), and going in-depth in your topic which you then \"teach\" to the other students in a 15-20 minute presentation. I found that the quality of the student presentations really varied; the short time you have to write the research paper and prepare the presentation means some of the quality suffers, but no doubt students put a lot of hours and effort into this class over the course of 12 weeks.", "It was a class where I deep dived into the topics that I had the papers assigned to. It was also a good opportunity to get a lot of presentation practice in. I also appreciated getting to know different people in the class. I got to develop the skills of using new research tools.", "The course touches on an overview of multiple aspects and contributors to agricultural production of feed, fuel, fiber & food systems. Most of the course is actually taught by the students giving bi-weekly presentations with groups and writing corresponding papers on assigned topics within the sector. reviews: ["I learned more about cinema history during this time than any other film history course I've taken. reviews: ["Some market failures, Coase, Information, public goods, voting, distribution", "We found solutions to internalise externalities, voting systems.", "We've used economic logic and tools to study how government intervention affects markets. reviews: ["A bit of coding.", "New research, technical, critical thinking, and analysis skills", "I have been working with Professor Michael Best's team. I've been primarily training this semester in how the team has been working with Python and SQL. I hope to gain more exposure into the research's progress and application next semester.", "SpinCo Abnormal Returns", "- History and Geography of New York city, Gentrification, Immigration, Financial Crisis, Literature Review", "Through the research I conducted with Rene Chalom I was able to learn a lot about subsidies in the Electric Charging space and their impact on Electric Vehicle demand and also the creation of additional charging stations, focusing on the West Coast, specifically in California. I was able to learn about the Low Carbon Fuel Standard policies in California. In my tasks as a research assistant I spent time researching on the laws California had imposed in-terms of LCFS and the generation of carbon credits in order to further incentivize building charging stations. Additionally, I specifically focused on Fast Charging Infrastructure (FCI credits) learning about the qualifications, legal and regulatory approvals needed to gain FCI credits from charging networks such as EVgo, Tesla, and EVconnect. I was then able to do thorough economic analysis learning about the impact the FCI credit system had from the charging network perspective and how the new credit system impacted the building of new charging networks in California. I also learned how to conduct in-depth data analysis via excel by conducting tasks such as the pricing schedules, station specific pricing, markups, and locations of charging stations and the changes in the pace of building charging stations after the regulation was implemented in California.", "My research was mostly a resume builder.", "- A good opportunity to explore your interests in economics research 1 Columbia University: Arts & Sciences Fall 2023 Course: ECONGU4996_001_2023_3-RESEARCHCOURSE : Fall 2023 CC Research Credit-ECONGU4996_001_2023_3 - RESEARCH COURSE Instructor: Susan Elmes"]

reviews: ["...", "The biggest transferable skill I'm taking away from this course is literature and poetry analysis. I think I'm a more thoughtful and questioning reader now - also, of course, I have a much greater appreciation for Chaucer and the extent of his contributions to the history of English literature.", "Middle English, historical context, Boethian Philosophy, and many literary experiments Chaucer put forward.", "I learned a tremendous about The Canterbury Tales, Chaucer, Medieval literature, frame tale narratives, and how it is possible for a collection of literature to cover so much variety and experimentation. This was also followed with a lot of attention to detail in close reading and analysis of tales in order to make arguments and meanings.", "We covered the entire Canterbury Tales and learned the sociocultural background behind the work.", "I learned how to interpret The Canterbury Tales and I learned a lot about the cultural context in which the Tales were written.", "I learned to differentiate the narrative's base meaning from its interpretive meaning, from the pilgrim's intentions from the pilgrim's psychology, from Chaucer's grand orchestration. There are too many layers of meaning. I also learned how a narrative can serve as a writer's practice of literary theory.", "All about the history and content of the Canterbury Tales", "The content itself was interesting alone, but Professor Johnson made the class. She was really engaging and enthusiastic, and her knowledge and opinions on the text felt refreshing and not stuffy, which I think some Medieval English classes can be sometimes. I definitely recommend taking it with her.", "This class is excellent. Professor Johnson's lectures are a treat. I definitely came out of this class with a greater appreciation for literature generally and the Canterbury Tales specifically.", "This class is fun and thought-provoking, Professor Johnson's energy WILL bring you closer to the text and the fascinating world of Chaucerian language and literary experiment.", "You should take any class that Professor Johnson offers every. However, I think the value in this class is that The Canterbury Tales is such a powerhouse in English literature, that getting to explore it in detail with Professor Johnson really allows you to reassess your approach to major entries to the English literature \"canon\" and find more beauty and experimental successes in such a well-known work. Absolutely worth every minute.", "I already said this in my assessment of the course, but I will repeat it: This course is absolutely incredible. The lectures were not only extremely interesting, but genuinely so funny and entertaining. I never minded waking up for the 8:40 class because once I was there, I was locked in. I loved how we approached each tale, and how thoroughly we explored each topic. The daily homework was never excessive-- only to read the tale we were going to discuss in class, which was the perfect amount of work. The two essays were always addressed in class and we had ample opportunity to discuss our ideas with Professor Johnson. It was so fun to learn about the Tales and there was so much juicy material to learn with each one-- every class was a like a revelation as Prof explained the fascinating meanings and subtexts that I would never have figured out on my own."]

reviews: ["You will learn about a multitude of dramas and theories as it applies to how theatre is performed and read.", "This is the kind of course that is life-changing. Professor Quigley's ability to deliver complex information in a comprehensible manner is impressive and noteworthy. If you like plays, you will be going back to Shakespearean times and comparing these plays such as Antony and Cleopatra to theorists such as Aristotle and Brecht. Your knowledge of history and history to know by and of will grow, and you will not only become a more critical thinker, but a better one. You will have a weekly response paper for each play/theorist in addition to two papers (a midterm paper about 5-6 pages on (in 2024) Antony and Cleopatra and theorists, a final paper about 10-12 pages on Monat/Sade and theorists). The plays themselves are complex, but Professor Quigley will do a great job at leading you through the content. My mind felt like it crinkled some more after his class. This is a class you must take, if not purely to be in a class with Professor Quigley. 1 Columbia University: Arts & Sciences Fall 2024 Course: ENTAUN3701_001_2024_3-DRAMA,THEATREANDTHEORY : ENTAUN3701_001_2024_3 - DRAMA, THEATRE AND THEORY Instructor: Austin Quigley"]

reviews: ["Wider knowledge of history relating to cinema", "I entered this class with little understanding of cinematic history between 1960-1990. reviews: ["I took this course to improve on my Chinese, especially to improve on my ability to communicate with others better in Chinese - potentially even in the workplace in future. reviews: ["It's really hard to say in a concise, structured way what I learned in this class. Because I learned so much, in so many dimiensions, that it's hard to even start. I'll just paste in part of a letter I wrote to the professor: \"I took many things in your class to heart. I loved our class conversations. I loved becoming friends with our classmates, whom it was so easy to befriend given the conversations we had in the class (see the attached photos!). Thank you for writing such encouraging and thought-provoking comments on my papers. When you told me I was a good writer, or said I should get a new sweater, or encouraged me to play different kinds of music \u2014 every comment has and will stick with me for a long time. I feel like you really took the time to understand what I was writing and saying, and also what I maybe did not have the courage to put into words. Because of that, I knew I could trust you, I knew that I could open up doors that I feared. reviews: ["This class is very much a \"drinking out of the firehouse\" class. So rather than knowledge, you are always swarmed by a need to do well. I took this class as I am very interested in genetics but with 4 midterms + a cumulative final - its just not worth it and probably killed any interest I had in the class in the first place. The instructors just glaze over the concepts in class and then expect you to somehow know everything during a time-crunch exam, which all just snowballs and isn't fun at all.", "approaches to genetics, bacterial genetics, methods of research, etc.", "General and more complex genetics concepts ex. recombination, pedigrees, mendelian inheritance, non-mendelian inheritance,", "Interesting class but taught in a not super engaging way. There is a lot of content that you need to know and explanations of practice problems are subpar so you have to teach yourself a majority.", "I probably wouldn't recommend this course to another student (if its not part of your requirement). There are four midterms, the final is cumulative, and there are almost bi-weekly take home quizzes. In terms of workload, this course is extremely time consuming, which is exacerbated by the fact that it is also very problem based. There is essentially 1 pset every week, and if you fail to go to the optional recitations to review them then they get very confusing. I was staying on top of the material the entire semester, and I pretty much never missed lecture, but even then I felt slightly overwhelmed by the amount of material and problems I had to complete. However, on the flip side, I really enjoyed the in-class activities and particularly the discussion about the ethics of Crispr/Cas9 as I feel like most STEM classes don't really take the time to discuss the implications of the material they are teaching. I definitely feel way more confident in genetics topics, and I particularly think that this course would be good for those who have a research interest in genetics, as opposed to a pre-medical interest.", "I would take this class if you need to for an upper-level course - but don't take it for fun. If you really like genetics that much, you can learn it online tbh and you'll get more out of it.", "Probably the least painful core Bio class!", "The subject itself is so interesting, and the course really makes me feel dissuaded from pursuing further studies in genetics", "The class is a lot of work and while they curve very well, I would not say that it is worth the effort unless one wants to go into genetics or requires this class.", "You will be stressed about the exams but there is a pretty generous curve"]

However, the TA's feedback was that we should have written about both objects at the same time, and when asked in class, the teacher said we should defer to the TA's opinion. The expectations regarding assignments like the essays were very unclear which made me enjoy the course a lot less. In addition, the midterm expectations were similarly wishy- washy, as there was no study guide provided, Instead, we were asked to study all art pieces presented in the class and try to fill in the information if we could find it, like the name of the piece or the date, and essentially guess which pieces would be most likely to be tested based on how much time she spent on discussing them. Overall, the class would be greatly improved by just providing more clear expectations, as the grading is a bit unfair. 1 Columbia University: Arts & Sciences Spring 2025 Course: AHISUN2600_001_2025_1-THEARTSOFCHINA : AHISUN2600_001_2025_1 - THE ARTS OF CHINA Instructor: Catherine Zhu"]

reviews: ["I learned a general approach to research genetics.", "This course gave me a deep and comprehensive understanding of genetics, far beyond what I expected. It was by far the best biology course I have taken so far.", "A lot. walked away with so much knoweldge about the foundations of genetics, genetic experiments, and applications.", "Tetrad analysis, pedigree analysis, mendelian and non mendelian analysis, mosaicism, genetic morphs, bacterial genetics", "Elementary elements of transmission and molecular genetics split over five units. reviews: ["So much about the basics of PR and applying the skills to current events. Just a great class. reviews: ["Public Relations & Corporate Comms teaches you how to shape & manage press coverage. We studied media monitoring, making story pitches, trendspotting, press releases, and building PR campaigns. It was super helpful to take this class during the Astronomer crisis (CEO + HR caught at Coldplay concert) & the American Eagle + Sydney Sweeney campaign fallout.", "I have gained valuable insights into how the PR world operates and the core knowledge required to navigate it successfully. This includes understanding the process of crafting and delivering effective pitches, managing and protecting an organization\u2019s reputation, and developing communication strategies that align with business objectives. Through these lessons, I have built a stronger foundation in both the creative and strategic aspects of public relations, preparing me to handle real-world challenges in the field.", "This course gave me so much more than just an overview of PR and corporate communications, it gave me practical, hands-on tools I can use immediately in my work. I learned how to take a story and shape it into something strategic, compelling, and press-ready. We covered how to work with the media in ways that build trust and generate meaningful coverage, manage a brand\u2019s reputation in the fast-moving world of social media, and navigate high-pressure crisis situations with confidence and credibility. One of my favorite parts was learning how to \u201cnewsjack\u201d\u2014spotting a timely moment in the news and figuring out how a company or brand could authentically and creatively join the conversation. It was a perfect blend of quick thinking, strategy, and storytelling. The guest speakers were phenomenal, each bringing different perspectives from corporate, nonprofit, government, and agency worlds. The assignments were the real game-changer\u2014they weren\u2019t \u201cfor a grade\u201d exercises, they were real-world deliverables that felt like the kind of work you\u2019d actually be asked to do in the field.", "I learned a lot of practical skills - how to send a pitch to a journalist, prepare a press release etc. As someone with little PR experience it was very useful", "I have 6+ years of PR and corporate communications experience and thought this class would take my skills/perspective to the next level. Unfortunately, it didn't. This felt like an undergraduate course, not a master's level PR and corporate communications course. While I know not every student enters the class with the same baseline experience, it felt like we never got bast the basics.", "This course gave me a deeper understanding of communications and corporate PR, especially how messaging strategies shift between corporate, consumer, and internal contexts. I gained skills in analyzing case studies, developing PR materials, and understanding the nuances of brand positioning in high-profile industries. I also improved my ability to think critically about how different audiences perceive communication and how reputation is managed in real time. At the same time, I learned how important it is to adapt PR frameworks to multiple industries, and how brand voice and stakeholder expectations shape communications strategy. While the workload was demanding for a summer session, it reinforced time management, discipline, and the ability to deliver under pressure."]
